structured_choices.data_source: Electronic Health Records (EHR) from a single local hospital system, spanning 5 years (60 months), containing 10,000 patients with temporal symptom records, lab results, clinical notes, and clinician-assessed severity scores at follow-up visits. Data quality issues: irregular visit intervals (range: 7–180 days), missing symptom entries (~12% sparse), variable clinician assessment practices (inter-rater reliability unknown), and incomplete treatment adherence documentation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Disease severity at the next clinical follow-up visit, defined as the clinician-assessed severity score (assumed continuous, 0–100 or similar ordinal scale converted to continuous). Labels obtained retrospectively from the EHR. Unknown severity assessments (missing follow-up visits) are excluded from analysis. Assessment timing must be ≥7 days after the preceding visit to ensure clinical meaningfulness.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with patient stratification: (1) Chronologically partition the 5-year dataset into three consecutive windows: months 1–36 (train, 60%), months 37–48 (validation, 20%), months 49–60 (test, 20%). This preserves the temporal dependency structure and prevents data leakage from future clinical visits. (2) Within each window, stratify patients by baseline disease severity quartile and comorbidity burden (derived from first visit in dataset) to ensure balanced representation of disease subtypes across splits. This controls for confounding by disease severity at baseline and ensures the test set is representative of the deployment population.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit on the training set only, then applied identically to validation and test sets. Specifically: (1) Forward-fill imputation for missing symptom values within patient trajectories is fit (parameters learned from training set forward-fill statistics), then applied to val/test. (2) Missing lab results are imputed using median values computed from the training set only. (3) Temporal features (days since last visit, visit frequency) are computed for each row independently; no leakage risk. (4) Clinical note text encodings (if used) are derived from a vocabulary and embedding fit on training notes only.
structured_choices.features: RNN model features: (1) Temporal sequence: symptom severity scores, lab result values, and vital signs recorded at each visit, ordered chronologically within each patient's trajectory. Sequence length varies (padded or truncated to max 24 visits per patient, ~2 years of history). (2) Temporal metadata: days elapsed since last visit (normalized), visit sequence index. (3) Static features: age at baseline, sex, primary diagnosis code, comorbidity count (Charlson index). (4) Clinician note features (optional): bag-of-words or pre-trained embedding of clinical assessment text from the visit preceding the prediction target. Baseline (logistic regression) features: static medical features only—age, sex, diagnosis, comorbidity count, and the severity score from the most recent visit (snapshot feature). No temporal information in baseline.
structured_choices.model: RNN model: a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) with 2 layers (64 hidden units each), trained to predict the severity score at the next visit. Input: temporal sequences of symptom/lab values per patient. Output: continuous severity prediction. Architecture justified: captures temporal dependencies, irregular visit intervals handled via attention or temporal encoding, bi-directionality captures both past and (within-patient) future context. Hyperparameters tuned on validation set (learning rate, dropout, sequence length, batch size).
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression (or linear regression, depending on severity scale) fit on static medical features only: age, sex, primary diagnosis, comorbidity count, and the most recent severity score. Baseline receives identical hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search on the validation set (regularization strength C, penalty type). Baseline is trained on the same training data (same patients, same time window), evaluated on identical train/val/test splits. This ensures the RNN advantage (if any) comes from modeling temporal dynamics, not from having more features or tuning.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the held-out test set, reported in the same units as the severity score. Justification: (1) Operational alignment—clinicians care about point-estimate accuracy; an error of ±5 severity points has interpretable clinical meaning. (2) Robust to outliers compared to MSE, appropriate given potential data entry errors in EHR. (3) Directly optimizable and interpretable. (4) No threshold tuning required (unlike classification metrics), avoiding arbitrary decision-making. MAE must be reported stratified by baseline disease severity quartile to detect heterogeneous performance.
secondary_metrics: Median Absolute Error (MdAE) — robustness to extreme outliers
Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) — penalizes larger errors, if clinicians have nonlinear cost functions
R² on test set — variance explained, contextualizes magnitude of MAE
MAE stratified by comorbidity burden and diagnosis subgroup — tests generalization across patient populations
Calibration plot (predicted vs. actual severity, binned) — assesses whether predicted values are well-calibrated for clinical use
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning (both RNN and baseline) is performed using the validation set only (months 37–48). A grid or random search over a pre-specified hyperparameter space is conducted; the model/baseline combination with the lowest validation MAE is selected. The test set (months 49–60) is touched only once, at the end, to report final performance. No information from the test set informs model selection.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out and untouched during all model development, feature engineering, and hyperparameter selection. Test set MAE (and secondary metrics) are computed exactly once, on the final trained model. Results are reported as point estimates with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling within the test set, 1000 iterations). No re-training, no adaptive thresholding, no additional tuning based on test performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Treatment adherence (known confound): Stratification by comorbidity burden and baseline severity is a proxy control, as adherence often correlates with these factors. Ideally, adherence data would be encoded as a feature in both models (if available in EHR), making it a covariate rather than a confound. Inter-rater reliability (clinician assessment variability): Compute Intraclass Correlation (ICC) or Cohen's kappa on a sample of double-assessments from the training set; if ICC < 0.7, consider consensus labels or restrict to high-agreement assessors. Baseline disease severity: controlled via stratified split (patients balanced across severity quartiles in train/val/test). Irregular visit intervals: normalized as a feature, not a source of leakage. Deployment population mismatch: The local hospital EHR may not generalize to other hospitals or healthcare systems; this study tests performance on the observed population only. External validation would require a separate prospective dataset from a different site.

design_narrative:
To test whether an RNN model predicts disease severity more accurately than logistic regression, we propose the following controlled experiment using the available 5-year EHR dataset.

**1. Data Preparation and Quality Assurance**

We will work with the full cohort of 10,000 patients and their temporal symptom, lab, and clinical note records spanning 60 months. Before proceeding, we must audit data quality: (1) Quantify the proportion and pattern of missing symptom and lab values; (2) Examine the distribution of visit intervals (median, range, skew) to understand the irregularity challenge; (3) Assess inter-rater reliability of clinician severity assessments by examining a stratified sample of patients with multiple assessments by different clinicians (target ICC ≥ 0.7; if lower, restrict to high-agreement clinicians or use consensus labels); (4) Document variability in treatment adherence documentation, noting which patients have complete records versus sparse adherence data.

**2. Data Splitting Strategy**

Temporality is critical: we split chronologically to prevent future information from leaking into training. The dataset is divided into three consecutive 20-month windows: months 1–36 form the training set (60%, ~6,000 patients with complete trajectories in this window), months 37–48 form the validation set (20%, ~2,000 patients), and months 49–60 form the held-out test set (20%, ~2,000 patients). Within each window, we stratify patients by baseline disease severity (quartile 1–4) and comorbidity burden (Charlson index: 0, 1–2, ≥3) to ensure all three splits have balanced representation of disease subtypes. This stratification controls for confounding by disease severity and comorbidity, and ensures the test set mirrors the demographic and clinical structure of the training population.

**3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing**

All preprocessing steps are fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test.

*RNN features:* We construct temporal sequences per patient, ordered chronologically. Each sequence includes the symptom severity, key lab result values, and vital signs at each visit. We standardize sequence length to 24 visits (approximately 2 years of history; patients with fewer visits are zero-padded; those with more are truncated from oldest records). We compute two temporal metadata features: (a) days elapsed since the prior visit (normalized by z-score, using training set mean and SD), and (b) visit sequence index (0–23). We add static baseline features: age, sex, primary diagnosis code (one-hot encoded), and Charlson comorbidity index. If clinical notes are used, we extract a bag-of-words vector or apply a pre-trained clinical text embedding (fit vocabulary on training notes only).

*Baseline (logistic regression) features:* Only static features—age, sex, diagnosis, Charlson index, and the severity score from the most recent visit. No temporal dynamics are available to the baseline.

*Missing data handling:* Within-patient missing symptom values are forward-filled using the median forward-fill imputation strategy learned from the training set. Missing lab results are imputed using the training set median (computed per lab type). Any patient-visit pair still lacking a severity label (target variable) is excluded from analysis.

**4. Model Architecture and Baseline**

The RNN is a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) with two stacked layers (64 hidden units per layer), dropout (0.3), and an output layer predicting continuous severity. Bidirectionality captures past and longitudinal context; temporal metadata features help the model handle irregular intervals. The baseline is logistic regression (or linear regression, if the severity scale is truly continuous) trained on static features only. Both models are given equal effort in hyperparameter tuning: grid search over learning rate (1e-3, 1e-2, 1e-1), batch size (16, 32, 64), dropout (0.2, 0.3, 0.4), and sequence length (12, 18, 24). Tuning is performed on the validation set; the configuration minimizing validation MAE is selected for both models.

**5. Evaluation Strategy**

*Model selection:* Hyperparameter tuning uses only the validation set (months 37–48). The model and baseline configurations with the lowest validation MAE are frozen. The test set is not examined during tuning.

*Primary metric:* Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set, reported in the original severity score units. MAE is clinically interpretable (e.g., an MAE of 3 means predictions are off by 3 points on average) and aligns with the stakeholder's need for accurate point estimates of severity.

*Secondary metrics:* (1) Median Absolute Error (MdAE), for robustness; (2) RMSE, if clinicians incur nonlinear costs for larger errors; (3) R² on test set, contextualizing the variance explained; (4) MAE stratified by baseline severity quartile and comorbidity group, detecting whether the RNN's advantage is consistent across patient subgroups; (5) Calibration plots comparing predicted to observed severity within decile bins, ensuring predictions are trustworthy for clinical decision-making.

**6. Confound and Bias Controls**

*Treatment adherence:* Patients with different adherence levels may have different symptom trajectories. We control for this via stratified splitting (ensuring balanced comorbidity and baseline severity across splits, both proxies for adherence). If adherence data is available in the EHR, we include it as a feature in both models, converting it from a confound to a covariate.

*Clinician assessment variability:* We measure inter-rater reliability (ICC) on a sample of patients with multiple clinician assessments. If ICC < 0.7, we restrict analyses to high-agreement clinicians or use consensus labels. This reduces label noise.

*Temporal validity:* Our split ensures no future information contaminates training. Validation and test sets consist of visits strictly after the training window, preserving causal ordering.

*Generalization scope:* This experiment validates performance on the local hospital's population during the 5-year period. Generalization to other hospitals or future years would require external validation on a separate prospective cohort.

**7. Interpretation and Claims**

If the RNN's test MAE is statistically significantly lower than the baseline's (bootstrapped 95% CI), we can conclude that **temporal modeling of symptom progression improves severity prediction accuracy on this dataset**. This result would establish that the RNN captures clinically relevant dynamics not captured by static features. However, this result is specific to the observed population and hospital system; external generalization requires prospective validation.
